Empires & Puzzles Killhare Review: The Savage Springvale Protector
Killhare was once the queen of punishment, dealing massive damage at a cost. Years later, does her Reckless Swing still land with the same impact, or has the meta hopped past her?
Killhare Review: Still A Savage Protector?
Killhare: The Savage Protector Who Hits Like a Truck (And Breaks Your Shield)
. In her prime, she was a terrifying force of nature—a bunny who didn't bring eggs, but annihilation. She is a
class hero, known for her "high risk, high reward" playstyle. While the game has evolved significantly since her debut, her raw damage output at
speed keeps her in the conversation for many rosters, though she now faces stiff competition from modern powerhouses.
Note: This review focuses on the
version of Killhare. A separate review covers her Costume.

Killhare’s design is a fantastic subversion of the Easter Bunny trope. Instead of a fluffy, basket-toting friend, we get a savage warrior with tribal markings, fierce eyes, and a massive sword that looks like it could cleave a tank in two. The "Savage Protector" moniker fits perfectly; she looks ready to defend her burrow by any means necessary. It's a refreshing change from the more whimsical Springvale designs—this bunny clearly chose violence.
By modern 2026 standards, Killhare's base stats show her age.
Once considered high, this is now well below the 1300+ attack stats seen on modern heroes like
These durability stats are now comparable to modern 4-star heroes (or even some 3-star costumes). Without Limit Breaks or emblems, she is incredibly fragile in the current meta.

significantly buffed the costume bonus (even for the base hero if you own the costume), providing a much-needed stat injection (+15% Atk/Def, +20% HP, +5% Mana). If you have her costume, her base stats become serviceable, though still not elite.
This is the headline. Hitting all 5 enemies for 300% at Average speed was groundbreaking in 2020. Today, it's still "good" damage, but heroes like
can output significantly more, often with fewer drawbacks.

A modern hitter with 1300 Attack dealing 300% would output ~3900 value. Killhare relies heavily on Emblems, Limit Breaks, and Troops to close this gap.

Base Killhare has no innate passives, a hallmark of older heroes.

In 2020/2021, Killhare was a top-tier defensive flank and offensive powerhouse. She punished passive boards and could wipe entire teams if left unchecked.

The introduction of heroes with 1300+ base power has pushed Killhare out of the top meta. Her damage percentage (300%) is no longer an outlier, and her drawback is harder to justify when modern heroes deal more damage with
She is no longer a viable defense hero in top-tier wars. However, she remains a
offensive option for mid-game players, particularly in
version is generally preferred for one specific reason:
JF's special skill flips "Defense Down" ailments into "Defense Up". If you fire JF then Base Killhare, her -20% Defense becomes a massive
buff for your team. This synergy does
work with the Costume (which drops Attack).
If you don't use JF, the Costume is generally better for the higher damage multiplier and slightly less punishing drawback (Attack Down is often survivable; Defense Down gets you killed).

path is the only way to go. If she fires, you want it to hurt.
300% to everyone is still a significant chunk of damage.
The ability to Revive with 1 HP and fire her special can turn a losing match into a win.
One of the best synergies in the game's history, turning her weakness into a strength.
The -20% Defense down is dangerous, essentially inviting the enemy to wipe your team if you don't kill them first.
Extremely low durability compared to modern heroes.
Without mana troops/bonuses, 10 tiles can be too slow in a "Very Fast" meta.

High damage output is king here, and healing drawbacks don't matter (though defense down still hurts).
JF protects the team from Killhare's debuff, flipping it to a buff. Rigard provides the Attack Up.
The classic "1-2-3" punch. Elemental Defense Down -> Defense Down -> Nuke. Few teams survive this sequence.
heroes love facing Killhare. If she hits a team with Boril/Cyprian/Elena active, she will almost certainly kill herself on the reflect.
Avoid pairing her with heroes that override defense buffs if you are relying on the JF combo.

If you pull Killhare early, she is a game-changer. She will carry you through map stages and lower-tier raids effortlessly. Don't worry too much about the defense down drawback in early game; the raw damage usually kills enemies before they can capitalize on it.

Killhare is likely a bench hero for you unless you are running a very specific "Defense Flip" team or need a deep roster for War Attacks. Do not spend Alpha Aethers on her unless you have a deep emotional attachment or lack modern AoE purples. The Costume Bonus update helped, but she is not a priority.
"Killhare is the most powerfull hero in the game. Period." -
A sentiment from 2020 that shows just how dominant she once was.
"The rabbit is insanely OP. No other AoE causes nowhere near this much damage..." -
Referencing a beta change where she was temporarily nerfed to 280% before being restored.

Killhare is a legend of the arena. While she may have lost a step to the younger, flashier heroes of 2025 and 2026, she can still swing that sword with devastating effect. She represents an era where heroes had distinct, meaningful drawbacks to balance their power. If you have the Jean-François combo, she remains a tactical nuke. If not, she's a dangerous glass cannon that demands respect.
A retired champion who can still knock you out if you let your guard down.
Is the original Killhare still worth your tabbards in 2026? We analyze her high-risk, high-reward gameplay, stats, and how she compares to modern meta heroes.
